/**
 * Add trusted origins to the global CSP.
 */
public function extendCSP($rules) {
    if (!in_array("https://maps.googleapis.com", $rules['script-src'])) {
        $rules['script-src'][] = "https://maps.googleapis.com";
    }
    return $rules;
}

[!CAUTION] Avoid using http:// for external assets. The core security policy blocks insecure sources by default to prevent mixed content vulnerabilities.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class — All available hooks.
  • Mod Class — Module utilities.
  • Mail Class — Send emails from modules.
  • Options Class — Store and retrieve module settings.
  • Token Class — CSRF protection for module forms.
  • Control Layer Reference — How module routes are dispatched.
  • Model Class — Base ORM class.

docs.title: Theme Development Guide
docs.text: GeniXCMS features a powerful, decoupled theme engine that allows you to craft high-performance frontend experiences. This guide provides a step-by-step walkthrough for building a custom theme from the ground up, leveraging the Latte templating engine and the integrated Hooks system.

docs.title: ⚡ Quick Start: Using the CLI
docs.text: The fastest way to start a new theme is by using the built-in genix console. This command automatically generates the required directory and all boilerplate files (header.php, footer.php, index.php, etc.).

docs.title: Generate Scaffolding
docs.text:
In your project root, run:

php genix make:theme my-awesome-theme

docs.title: Activate the Theme
docs.text:
Once generated, you can instantly activate it via the CLI:

php genix theme:activate my-awesome-theme

docs.title: 🏗️ Theme Architecture
docs.text: Themes are located in inc/themes/{your-theme-name}/. A well-structured theme follows this standardized file layout:

docs.title: 📂 Core Required Files
docs.text: File Role themeinfo.php Metadata: Defines theme name, version, and developer info. header.php Head Section: Contains <head>, meta tags, and top navigation. footer.php Tail Section: Contains footer layout and closing tags. index.php Homepage: The default template for the root URL. single.php Post View: The template for individual blog posts. css/style.css Styling: The default stylesheet loaded automatically by OptionsBuilder.

docs.title: 📂 Logic & Customization
docs.text:
• function.php: Optional logic file to register hooks, load assets, and define theme classes.
  • options.php: Optional admin panel for theme-specific settings.

docs.title: 🚀 Step 1: Defining Theme Metadata (themeinfo.php)
docs.text:
GeniXCMS parses the comment block in this file to display theme information in the administration dashboard.

<?php
/*
 * Name: Modern Nexus
 * Desc: A clean, performance-optimized theme for GeniXCMS 2.0.
 * Version: 1.0.0
 * Developer: GeniXCMS Team
 * URI: https://genixcms.web.id
 * License: MIT License
 * Icon: bi bi-palette
 */

docs.title: 🎨 Step 2: The Shell (header.php & footer.php)
docs.text: GeniXCMS uses Latte, which means variables are accessible via {$variable} and logic via {if} or {foreach}.

docs.title: header.php
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="{$website_lang}">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>{$sitetitle|noescape} - {$site_name}</title>
    {$site_meta|noescape}
    {Site::loadLibHeader()|noescape}
</head>
<body>
<nav class="navbar navbar-expand-lg">
    <div class="container">
        <a class="navbar-brand" href="{$site_url}">{$site_logo|noescape}</a>
        {Menus::getMenu('main')|noescape}
    </div>
</nav>

docs.title: footer.php
docs.text:
<footer class="bg-dark text-white py-4 mt-5">
    <div class="container text-center">
        {$site_footer|noescape}
    </div>
</footer>
{* Critical: Required to load system & module scripts *}
{Site::loadLibFooter()|noescape}
</body>
</html>

[!IMPORTANT] Don't Forget the Hooks: Always include {Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape} before </body>. Without it, core features like AJAX, popups, and module scripts will fail.

docs.title: 📝 Step 3: Content Templates (index.php)
docs.text:
The homepage template receives the $posts array automatically.

<main class="container py-5">
    <div class="row">
        {foreach $posts as $post}
        <div class="col-md-4 mb-4">
            <div class="card h-100 shadow-sm">
                <div class="card-body">
                    <h2 class="h5">
                        <a href="{Url::post($post->id)}">{$post->title|noescape}</a>
                    </h2>
                    <p class="text-muted small">
                         By {$post->author} | {Date::format($post->date)|noescape}
                    </p>
                    <p class="card-text">
                        {Posts::excerpt($post->content, 120)|noescape}
                    </p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>
        {/foreach}
    </div>
    <div class="d-flex justify-content-center mt-4">
        {$paging|noescape}
    </div>
</main>

docs.title: 🔧 Step 4: Theme Logic (function.php)
docs.text:
Use this file to register assets and hooks. It's best practice to wrap your logic in a Theme Class.

<?php
class NexusTheme {
    public function __construct() {
        // Register all theme-specific assets
        $this->registerAssets();

        // Define widget areas for this theme
        Widget::addLocation('nexus_header_top', 'Top Header Area');
        Widget::addLocation('nexus_sidebar_left', 'Left Sidebar');
    }

    private function registerAssets() {
        // Enqueue pre-defined system assets (jQuery is core)
        Asset::enqueue(['bootstrap-css', 'bootstrap-js', 'bootstrap-icons']);

        // Register & Enqueue Theme-specific CSS (loads in header by default)
        Asset::register('nexus-style', 'css', Url::theme() . '/assets/css/style.css', 'header');
        Asset::enqueue('nexus-style');

        // Register & Enqueue Theme-specific JS (loads in footer)
        Asset::register('nexus-script', 'js', Url::theme() . '/assets/js/theme.js', 'footer', ['jquery']);
        Asset::enqueue('nexus-script');
    }
}
new NexusTheme();

docs.title: 🧩 Step 5: Rendering Widgets
docs.text:
Once you've registered your custom widget locations in function.php, users will be able to place blocks there via the GeniXCMS Admin Dashboard (Appearance > Widgets).

To render these dynamic blocks into your theme designs, use Widget::show():

<div class="row">
    <!-- Left Sidebar pulling widgets assigned to 'nexus_sidebar_left' -->
    <aside class="col-md-3">
        {Widget::show('nexus_sidebar_left')|noescape}
    </aside>

    <!-- Main Content Area -->
    <main class="col-md-9">
        ...
    </main>
</div>

docs.title: 🛠️ Latte Syntax Quick Reference
docs.text: Action Syntax Print Variable {$var \| noescape} (Escaping is automatic by default) Include File {include 'sidebar.latte'} Looping {foreach $items as $item} ... {/foreach} Conditional {if $count > 0} ... {elseif $count == 0} ... {/if} Editor Setup {Theme::editor('full', '300') | noescape} PHP Method {ClassName::method($params) | noescape}

docs.title: 🔒 Handling Security (CSP)
docs.text:
If your theme loads external scripts from a third-party CDN, you must whitelist the domain using the system_security_headers_args hook:

Hooks::attach('system_security_headers_args', function($rules) {
    // Whitelist a new CDN for scripts
    $rules['script-src'][] = "https://cdn.example.com";
    return $rules;
});

docs.title: ⚙️ Step 6: Theme Options & Dynamic CSS
docs.text: If you want to offer an administrative panel for users to customize your theme (colors, typography, etc.), GeniXCMS provides the OptionsBuilder engine. It creates a robust UI in the backend and dynamically generates CSS variables for the frontend.

docs.title: 1. Registering the Admin Menu
docs.text:
Add a child menu item in your function.php constructor so users can access your theme's options:

AdminMenu::addChild('themes', [
    'label'  => _('Nexus Options'),
    'url'    => 'index.php?page=themes&view=options',
    'icon'   => 'bi bi-sliders',
    'access' => 0,
]);

docs.title: 2. Rendering Dynamic CSS
docs.text:
To output the customized styles into the <head>, hook into header_load_lib.

Critical Rule: You must return the CSS string instead of echoing it, to ensure it gets captured correctly by Site::loadLibHeader(). If your theme provides its own CSS file (e.g., assets/css/blog.css) and you don't want OptionsBuilder to auto-inject a <link> to style.css, pass 'loadStyleCss' => false.

Hooks::attach('header_load_lib', [__CLASS__, 'loadThemeCSS']);

public static function loadThemeCSS() {
    $css = '';

    // Generate dynamic CSS based on saved options
    if (class_exists('OptionsBuilder')) {
        $css .= OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS(self::$opt, [
            'themeUrl' => Url::theme(),
            'minify' => true,
            'loadStyleCss' => false // Set to false if you manually load your CSS files
        ]);
    }

    // Add any custom inline CSS here
    $css .= "<style>\n";
    $css .= "  :root { --nexus-color: " . (self::$opt['custom_color'] ?? '#000') . "; }\n";
    $css .= "</style>\n";

    // Return the string to keep the HTML structured properly
    return $css;
}

docs.title: 📱 Step 7: Dynamic Page Layouts
docs.text: GeniXCMS 2.1.1+ introduces a robust Dynamic Page Layout system. This feature allows theme developers to provide multiple design options for a single page (e.g., Landing Page, Fullwidth, Sidebar-Left, or Blank) which the end-user can select via a dropdown in the Admin Dashboard.

docs.title: 📂 Pro-Developer Naming Conventions
docs.text:
The system uses a strict naming pattern to automatically discover and link templates. All files must reside in your theme's root directory:

Template Type File Pattern Role Main View layout-{slug}.latte Replaces the entire page.latte content area. Custom Header header-{slug}.latte Replaces header.latte when this layout is active. Custom Footer footer-{slug}.latte Replaces footer.latte when this layout is active.

[!NOTE] {slug} is the unique identifier you choose (e.g., landing, blank, fullwidth).

docs.title: 🏗️ Creating a Registerable Layout
docs.text:
To make your layout appear in the Admin Panel with a human-readable name, add a specially formatted comment on the first line of your layout-{slug}.latte file.

Example: layout-landing.latte 

{* Layout: Premium Landing Page *}
<div class="landing-hero bg-primary text-white p-5">
    <h1 class="display-1">{$title}</h1>
    <div class="lead">{$content|noescape}</div>
</div>
If no comment is found, GeniXCMS will generate a name from the slug (e.g., "landing" becomes "Landing").

docs.title: 🔗 Technical Execution & Lookup Order
docs.text:
When a user visits a page assigned to a custom layout (e.g., slug = "blank"), the BaseControl::render() engine follows this precise lookup priority:

  1. Header Search:
    • Look for header-blank.latte (or .php).
    • Fallback: Use the standard header.latte.
  2. View Search:
    • Look for layout-blank.latte (or .php).
    • Fallback: Use the standard page.latte.
  3. Footer Search:
    • Look for footer-blank.latte (or .php).
    • Fallback: Use the standard footer.latte.

docs.title: 💡 Use Case: The "Blank" Workspace
docs.text:
Commonly used for pages inside iframes or landing pages that shouldn't have site navigation.

1. Create header-blank.latte (Minimalist head section): 

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    {$site_meta|noescape}
    {Site::loadLibHeader()|noescape}
</head>
<body class="bg-white">

2. Create layout-blank.latte (Just the content): 

{* Layout: Clean Workspace *}
<main class="container py-3">
    {$content|noescape}
</main>

3. Create footer-blank.latte (Minimalist scripts): 

{Site::loadLibFooter()|noescape}
</body>
</html>

docs.title: 🛠️ Variable Access
docs.text:
Any custom layout file has full access to the standard GeniXCMS data object, including: - {$title}: The page title. - {$content}: The main content (rendered HTML). - {$data['post']}: The full post object. - {$site_name}, {$site_url}, etc.

[!TIP] Pro Tip: Use the Vite Helper to integrate modern tools like Tailwind CSS and SCSS into your theme development workflow.

docs.title: Creating RESTful API Services
docs.text: GeniXCMS features a modular API dispatching system that allows any module to expose its own RESTful endpoints without modifying the core system. The system automatically routes requests based on class naming conventions.

docs.title: 🏗️ The API Architecture
docs.text:
GeniXCMS API follows a standard versioned structure: https://your-site.com/api/v1/{resource}/{identifier}

When a request is made to this URL, the Api class attempts to find and instantiate a handler class named {Resource}Api.

docs.title: 🛠️ Registering a Custom API Service
docs.text: To create an API service for your module, you simply need to define a class that follows the naming convention.

docs.title: 1. Define the API Class
docs.text:
Create a class in your module (e.g., in YourModule.lib.php or directly in init hook) named after your resource plus the Api suffix.

class MarketplaceApi
{
    // If true, no API Key / Token is required for GET requests
    public $is_public = true;

    /**
     * Handle GET requests
     * URL: /api/v1/marketplace/ OR /api/v1/marketplace/123
     */
    public function index($id = null)
    {
        if ($id) {
            $data = Posts::fetch(['id' => $id, 'type' => 'marketplace']);
            return Api::success($data, 'Item retrieved');
        } else {
            $data = Posts::lists(['type' => 'marketplace', 'num' => 10]);
            return Api::success($data, 'List of items');
        }
    }

    /**
     * Handle POST requests (Create)
     * URL: /api/v1/marketplace/
     */
    public function submit()
    {
        // Access JSON input
        $input = json_decode(file_get_contents('php://input'), true);

        // Process logic...

        return Api::success($input, 'Item created successfully');
    }

    /**
     * Handle PUT/PATCH requests (Update)
     * URL: /api/v1/marketplace/123
     */
    public function update($id)
    {
        return Api::success(['id' => $id], 'Item updated');
    }

    /**
     * Handle DELETE requests
     * URL: /api/v1/marketplace/123
     */
    public function delete($id)
    {
        return Api::success(['id' => $id], 'Item deleted');
    }
}

docs.title: 2. Loading the Class
docs.text:
The most efficient way to ensure your API handler is available when the API dispatcher calls it is by using spl_autoload_register in your module's entry file. This automatically discover and loads your classes as needed.

// In your module's main entry file
function loadModLib($class_name)
{
    // Search for the class file in your module's 'inc' directory
    Mod::inc($class_name.".class", '', dirname(__FILE__)."/inc/");
}
spl_autoload_register('loadModLib');

By following this pattern, files like MarketplaceApi.class.php located in your module's inc/ folder will be instantly found and instantiated by Api::dispatch() without any manual include statements.

docs.title: 🛡️ Authentication & Security
docs.text:
By default, all API endpoints are Private and require a valid API Key.

  1. Mandatory Key: Clients must send the key via the GX-API-KEY header or ?api_key= query parameter. Use Options::v('api_key') to find your site's key.
  2. $is_public property: If you set public $is_public = true; in your API class, the system will allow GET requests without authentication. POST/PUT/DELETE always require authentication for security reasons.
  3. Rate Limiting: Custom API services automatically inherit the system's rate limiting settings configured in the admin dashboard.

docs.title: 📤 Sending Responses
docs.text:
Use the helper methods in the Api class to ensure consistent JSON formatting:

  • Api::success($data, $message): Returns a 200 OK response with a success status.
  • Api::error($code, $message): Returns a specific HTTP error code and message.
  • Api::response($data, $code): General purpose method for any custom response.

[!TIP] Pro Tip: You can combine custom API services with Programmable Routing to create even more expressive URLs like /api/v1/marketplace/search/query by adding a custom route that maps to your API class.

docs.title: Custom Post Types
docs.text: GeniXCMS allows you to define and manage custom post types beyond the standard 'post' and 'page'. This is useful for creating specialized content like Books, Portfolio items, or Products.

docs.title: 🏗️ Creating a Custom Post Type Form
docs.text:
To access the editor for a custom post type, you use the standard posts management URL with an additional type query parameter.

Admin URL: gxadmin/index.php?page=posts&act=add&type=your_type_name

When you save a post through this URL, GeniXCMS automatically sets its type in the database to your_type_name.

docs.title: Customizing the Form with Hooks
docs.text: There are two ways to add custom fields to your post types: using raw Hooks (for full HTML control) or using the Params::register() system (standardized and faster).

docs.title: Method 1: Using Raw Hooks
docs.text:
You can inject any custom HTML into the post form using these hooks: - post_param_form_bottom: Injects below the main content editor. - post_param_form_sidebar: Injects at the bottom of the right sidebar.

Hooks::attach('post_param_form_bottom', function() {
    global $data;
    $post = $data[0]['post'][0] ?? null;
    $price = ($post) ? Posts::getParam('price', $post->id) : '';

    // Only show for documentation post type
    if (isset($data[0]['post_type']) && $data[0]['post_type'] === 'documentation') {
        echo '
        <div class="mb-3">
            <label>Price</label>
            <input type="text" name="param[price]" value="'.$price.'" class="form-control text-dark fw-bold">
        </div>';
    }
});

docs.title: Method 2: Using Params::register() (Recommended)
docs.text:
This method allows you to define a schema, and GeniXCMS will handle the rendering and metadata synchronization automatically.

Hooks::attach('init', function() {
    $vars['bottom'][] = [
        'grouptitle' => 'Documentation Properties',
        'groupname' => 'docs_settings',
        'post_type' => 'documentation', // Show only on documentation post type
        'fields' => [
            [
                'title' => 'Version Number',
                'name' => 'doc_version',
                'post_type' => 'documentation', // Optional: Show only for this post type
                'boxclass' => 'col-6',
                'type' => 'text'
            ],
            [
                'title' => 'Release Status',
                'name' => 'doc_status',
                'boxclass' => 'col-6',
                'type' => 'dropdown',
                'value' => ['beta', 'stable', 'deprecated']
            ],
            [
                'title' => 'Brief Technical Notes',
                'name' => 'doc_notes',
                'boxclass' => 'col-12',
                'type' => 'textarea'
            ]
        ]
    ];

    // Params::register will automatically use post_param_form_bottom hooks
    Params::register($vars);
});

[!NOTE] The 'post_type' property is optional. If you omit it, the fields will appear on all post types (posts, pages, and custom types).

Available field types: text, textarea, checkbox, dropdown.

GeniXCMS controllers automatically loop through any POST data named param[...] and save them as Post Parameters (metadata) for that record. You don't need to write custom save logic if you follow the name="param[key_name]" naming convention.

docs.title: 🎨 Frontend Rendering
docs.text:
By default, custom post types use the single.php (or single.latte) template in your theme. To provide a unique design for your post type:

  1. Create a file named your_type_name.php (or .latte) in your theme directory.
  2. The PostControl will automatically detect this file and use it instead of single.php.

docs.title: 🏗️ Customizing Category Layouts
docs.text:
Unlike single posts, the category controller (CatControl) defaults to a single cat.php or cat.latte view. To provide a unique look for categories belonging to a specific post type, use conditional logic inside your cat template:

{if $type == 'book'}
    <!-- Specialized Book Gallery Layout -->
    <div class="book-grid"> ... </div>
{else}
    <!-- Default Category List -->
    <div class="post-list"> ... </div>
{/if}

docs.title: 📂 Managing Categories for Custom Types
docs.text:
Categories in GeniXCMS are also type-aware. To manage a separate taxonomy for your custom post type:

Admin URL: gxadmin/index.php?page=categories&type=your_type_name

When you create a category here, it is assigned to your_type_name. In the post editor (with the correct type param), the category dropdown will automatically filter to show only categories belonging to that type.

docs.title: 🔍 Search Integration
docs.text:
By default, the frontend search only looks through 'post' types. To include your custom types in search results, use the search_type_filter hook:

Hooks::attach('search_type_filter', function($types) {
    $types[] = 'book';
    $types[] = 'portfolio';
    return $types;
});

docs.title: 🔗 URL Generation
docs.text: Use Url::post($id) for any record. The system will resolve the correct permalink regardless of the post type.

docs.title: Programmable Routing (On-the-fly)
docs.text: GeniXCMS allows modules to register custom URL routes dynamically using the Router::add() method. This is essential for modules that need to handle custom URL structures without relying on the default post or page routing.

docs.title: 🏗️ Registering Custom Routes
docs.text: To add a route, you should call Router::add() during your module's initialization phase (typically within the init hook). The method accepts an associative array where the key is a Regular Expression representing the URL path, and the value is an array defining the parameters to be extracted.

docs.title: Example: Documentation Module Routes
docs.text:
The following example demonstrates how to register routes for a documentation system that supports nested paths and .md extensions.

Hooks::attach('init', function() {
    Router::add([
        // Pattern for documentation posts with optional nested categories
        'docs/(?:(.*)/)?([^/]+)' . GX_URL_PREFIX => ['post' => 2, 'post_type' => 'documentation'],

        // Pattern for direct Markdown file access
        'docs/(?:(.*)/)?([^/]+).md' => ['post' => 2, 'post_type' => 'documentation'],

        // Main documentation explorer index
        'docs/' => ['mod' => 'docs'],
    ]);
});

docs.title: 🧪 Regex Pattern Rules
docs.text:
GeniXCMS uses preg_match with the Us (Ungreedy) modifier to match URIs.

  1. Capturing Groups: Each set of parentheses (...) creates a numbered match variable ($1, $2, etc.) that can be mapped to parameters.
  2. GX_URL_PREFIX: Always include this at the end of your pattern if you want to support the system-wide URL suffix (e.g., .html or /).
  3. Non-Capturing Groups: Use (?:...) if you need to group parts of the regex (like optional segments) without creating a match number.

docs.title: 🗺️ Parameter Mapping
docs.text:
The value array in Router::add() tells the system how to interpret the URL:

  • ['post' => 2]: Tells the controller that the 2nd captured group from the regex is the post slug/ID.
  • ['post_type' => 'documentation']: Hardcodes a parameter value (useful for filtering in the controller).
  • ['mod' => 'docs']: Routes the request to the docs module frontend controller.
  • ['lang' => 1]: Maps the 1st group to the system language (useful for en/path/to/content structures).

docs.title: 🚥 Processing Priority
docs.text:
The Router automatically sorts all registered routes by their string length in descending order (SORT_DESC). This ensures that more specific patterns (e.g., docs/sub/item/) are matched before more generic ones (e.g., docs/).

[!TIP] Data Extraction: Inside your controller, you can retrieve these mapped values using Router::scrap($param).

docs.title: 🏗️ Handling Different Post Type Layouts
docs.text: When you map a route to a post_type, you can use that information in your theme to load a specific layout. This allows you to differentiate how standard posts look vs. specialized module posts.

docs.title: Example: Loading Documentation Layout
docs.text:
In your theme's single.latte (or similar), you can check the post_type passed by the Router to include a specialized template:

{if isset($post_type) && $post_type === $posts[0]->type && $posts[0]->type === 'documentation'}
    {include 'layouts/docs_item.latte'}
{else}
    <!-- Regular Post Layout -->
    {include 'layouts/post_item.latte'}
{/if}

docs.title: Automatic File-Based Specialized Layouts
docs.text:
Beyond using conditional logic inside a single file, GeniXCMS's PostControl has a built-in mechanism to automatically load a file matching your post_type name.

If you have a route that sets post_type => 'documentation', GeniXCMS will look for:

  1. documentation.latte (or .php) in your theme directory.
  2. If that file exists, it will be used instead of the default single.latte.

This approach is much cleaner for complex custom post types as it separates the layout logic into dedicated files:

  • single.latte: For standard blog posts.
  • documentation.latte: For your documentation system.
  • portfolio.latte: For portfolio entries.

This pattern ensures that when a user visits a path matched by the docs/ route, they get the documentation-specific UI elements.

docs.title: Programmable Custom URLs
docs.text: GeniXCMS provides a flexible way to define custom URL structures for your modules. While the system has built-in URL generation for posts and pages, you can override these or create entirely new structures for your module's entities (like products, forum threads, or profile pages).

docs.title: 🏗️ Implementing a Custom URL Method
docs.text: When building a module, it is best practice to create a static method within your module's class (e.g., Marketplace::url($id)) that encapsulates the logic for both Smart URL and Standard URL formats.

docs.title: Example: Marketplace Item URL
docs.text:
The following example demonstrates how to generate a URL that routes through a specific folder (e.g., /modules/ or /themes/) based on the post type.

public static function item($vars)
{
    // Retrieve basic info from database
    $q = Query::table('posts')->select('slug, type')->where('id', Typo::int($vars))->first();
    if (!$q) {
        return Url::post($vars); // Fallback if record doesn't exist
    }

    $slug = $q->slug;
    $type = $q->type;
    $folder = ($type === 'marketplace_module') ? 'modules' : 'themes';

    switch (SMART_URL) {
        case true:
            $inFold = (Options::v('permalink_use_index_php') == 'on') ? 'index.php/' : '';
            if (Options::v('multilang_enable') === 'on') {
                $lang = Language::isActive();
                $lang = !empty($lang) ? $lang.'/' : '';
                $url = Site::$url.$inFold.$lang.$folder.'/'.$slug.GX_URL_PREFIX;
            } else {
                $url = Site::$url.$inFold.$folder.'/'.$slug.GX_URL_PREFIX;
            }
            break;

        default:
            if (Options::v('multilang_enable') === 'on') {
                $lang = Language::isActive();
                $lang = !empty($lang) ? '&lang='.$lang : '';
                $url = Site::$url."?post={$vars}{$lang}";
            } else {
                $url = Site::$url."?post={$vars}";
            }
            break;
    }

    return $url;
}

docs.title: 🔗 Overriding System-Wide Post URLs
docs.text: If your module uses a custom post type and you want Url::post($id) to automatically return your custom URL format, you should use the post_url filter hook.

docs.title: Registering the Override
docs.text:
Inside your module's init or constructor:

Hooks::attach('post_url', function($data) {
    // $data contains ['url' => ..., 'id' => ...]
    $post_id = $data['id'];
    $type = Posts::type($post_id);

    if ($type == 'marketplace_module' || $type == 'marketplace_theme') {
        // Return our custom URL structure
        $data['url'] = Marketplace::item($post_id);
    }

    return $data;
});

docs.title: 🛠️ Key Components for Custom URLs
docs.text:
When building custom URL logic, always consider these variables:

  1. SMART_URL: A global constant indicating if friendly URLs are enabled.
  2. index.php/ Prefix: Check Options::v('permalink_use_index_php') to support servers without .htaccess rewriting.
  3. Language Context: Use Language::isActive() to prepend the current language code if multilang_enable is 'on'.
  4. GX_URL_PREFIX: A global constant for the URL suffix (e.g., .html or /).

docs.title: 🚦 Routing Consideration
docs.text:
Defining a custom URL structure is only half of the task. You must also ensure that the Router knows how to handle these incoming paths. Custom paths often require a custom route definition in your module's init hook or via Router::add().

[!IMPORTANT] Always ensure your custom URL logic provides a fallback to standard GeniXCMS query parameters (?post=ID) when SMART_URL is disabled, to maintain compatibility across all hosting environments.

docs.title: Dynamic Builder Developer Guide
docs.text: This document describes how to extend and integrate the Dynamic Builder module for GeniXCMS from a technical perspective.

docs.title: Integration Architecture
docs.text: The Dynamic Builder is implemented as a GeniXCMS module using the GrapesJS framework. It is registered in the admin dashboard through AdminMenu::add() and is accessible at: index.php?page=mods&mod=dynamic-builder.

docs.title: Key Technical Components
docs.text:
• Core Module: Located at inc/mod/dynamic-builder/.
  • Primary Logic: JavaScript found at assets/js/builder.js.
  • Layout Management: layout.php handles the initialization and CSS/JS injection.
  • Canvas Styles: Custom CSS for the editor iframe is defined in assets/css/canvas.css.

docs.title: Integration via Hooks
docs.text:
The module is integrated into the core Page management system via standard GeniXCMS Hooks:

  • admin_footer_action: Used to load the editor's modal layout and dependency scripts only on the specific Add/Edit Page routes.
  • page_param_form_bottom: Used to inject the primary UI trigger ("Launch Visual Editor") at the bottom of the Page form.
  • header_load_lib: Enqueues necessary CSS and Google Fonts by scanning the page content for specific font families.
  • footer_load_lib: Loads standard frontend JavaScript required for interactive components (like carousels and counters).

docs.title: Extending the Builder
docs.text: The builder.js file contains all the configurations for blocks, component types, and commands.

docs.title: 1. Registering Custom Blocks
docs.text:
To add a new Block to the sidebar, use the getPremiumBlocks function or append directly to the GrapesJS BlockManager:

bm.add('my-custom-block', {
    label: 'Custom Block Title',
    category: 'Your Category',
    content: {
        type: 'my-custom-type',
        components: [{ /* initial DOM structure */ }]
    }
});

docs.title: 2. Defining Component Types
docs.text:
Use editor.DomComponents.addType to define custom behaviors and traits for your elements:

editor.DomComponents.addType('my-custom-type', {
    model: {
        defaults: {
            name: 'Custom Component',
            draggable: true,
            droppable: true,
            traits: [
                { type: 'text', label: 'Custom Attribute', name: 'data-custom' }
            ],
            // Scripts to run inside the canvas
            script: function() {
                console.log('Component initialized in canvas');
            }
        }
    }
});

docs.title: 3. Implementing Toolbar Commands
docs.text:
Extend a component's functionality with custom toolbar buttons and commands:

editor.Commands.add('my-custom-command', {
    run(editor, sender) {
        const selected = editor.getSelected();
        if (selected) {
            // Perform DOM manipulation or logic here
            selected.append('<div>New element</div>');
        }
    }
});

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Bootstrap 5 Classes: Ensure all blocks use standard Bootstrap 5 classes to maintain compatibility with the main GeniXCMS core styling.
  • Component Specificity: Use type: 'text' for editable text elements and type: 'image' for images to ensure they are correctly handled by GrapesJS's built-in managers.
  • Isolate Canvas Styles: If your components require custom styling, add it to canvas.css to ensure it is rendered within the editor's iframe.
  • Serialization: The builder synchronizes content back to a standard textarea in the GeniXCMS Pages or Posts admin view, including both the generated HTML and a <style> block containing the component CSS.

docs.title: CSS Implementation for Ratios
docs.text:
Custom aspect ratios (like 3:4 for team photos) should be defined in canvas.css to be correctly rendered within the editor:

.ratio-3x4 {
    --bs-aspect-ratio: 133.33%;
}

docs.title: Data Serialization
docs.text:
The save-builder-page command handles the data synchronization:

  1. It extracts editor.getHtml() and editor.getCss().
  2. Consolidates them into a single string: <style>...</style><html>...</html>.
  3. Sets the value of the primary GeniXCMS editor textarea for persistence.

docs.title: How to Configure Smart URLs on Apache and Nginx
docs.text:
Enabling Smart URLs (Permalinks) allows you to clean up your website's URL structure. Instead of dynamic query strings like /?post=my-post, you will have user-friendly and SEO-friendly links like /my-post.html or /my-post/.

To make this work seamlessly, both GeniXCMS and your web server need to be configured correctly.

docs.title: 1. Enable Smart URL within GeniXCMS
docs.text:
Unlike other settings, the Smart URL engine must be activated manually within your system's configuration file.

  1. Open your code editor and navigate to inc/config/config.php.
  2. Locate the SMART_URL definition (usually around line 28).
  3. Change its value from false to true: 
    !defined('SMART_URL') ? define('SMART_URL', true) : null;
  4. Save the file.

docs.title: 2. Apache Configuration (.htaccess)
docs.text:
If you are using Apache, the routing engine depends on the mod_rewrite module. GeniXCMS provides a default .htaccess file in the root directory that handles this automatically, but if you need to create or verify it manually, ensure the following core rewrite rules are present:

# 5. Core Rewrite Rules
Options +SymLinksIfOwnerMatch
RewriteEngine On
RewriteBase /

# Prevent script injection through URL
RewriteCond %{QUERY_STRING} (<|%3C).*script.*(>|%3E) [NC,OR]
RewriteCond %{QUERY_STRING} GLOBALS(=|\[|\%[0-9A-Z]{0,2}) [OR]
RewriteCond %{QUERY_STRING} _REQUEST(=|\[|\%[0-9A-Z]{0,2})
RewriteRule ^.*$ - [F,L]

# Route all traffic to index.php if the requested file/directory doesn't physically exist
RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-f
RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-d
RewriteRule . /index.php [L]

[!IMPORTANT] Make sure that AllowOverride All is permitted in your Apache VirtualHost so that the .htaccess file is recognized.

docs.title: 3. Nginx Configuration (nginx.conf)
docs.text:
If you are running Nginx, .htaccess files are ignored. You must configure the rewrite rules directly in your site's server block (nginx.conf).

The standard approach is to use try_files within the core routing location. Additionally, because dynamically generated thumbnails contain image extensions (.png, .jpg), you must ensure they bypass static file caching rules.

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name yourdomain.com;
    root /var/www/genixcms;
    index index.php index.html index.htm;

    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    # 1. Core Routing
    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    location / {
        try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string;
    }

    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    # 2. Dynamic Thumbnails Rewrite
    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Prevents /thumb/ requests (which end in .jpg, .png, etc.) from being 
    # intercepted by the static assets caching block below.
    location ^~ /thumb/ {
        try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string;
    }

    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    # 3. Static Assets Caching
    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    location ~* \.(jpg|jpeg|png|gif|ico|css|js|pdf|woff|woff2|ttf|svg)$ {
        expires 30d;
        add_header Cache-Control "public, no-transform";
        access_log off;
    }

    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    # 4. PHP Processing
    # ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    location ~ \.php$ {
        fastcgi_pass unix:/var/run/php/php-fpm.sock;
        fastcgi_index index.php;
        fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $document_root$fastcgi_script_name;
        include fastcgi_params;
    }
}

[!TIP] After altering your nginx.conf file, always check the syntax using nginx -t and restart Nginx with systemctl reload nginx.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Getting a 404 Not Found across all pages? This usually means URL routing isn't reaching index.php. Check that your .htaccess file was successfully copied, or verify your Nginx try_files block.
  • Thumbnails (/thumb/assets/...) returning a 404 on Nginx? Ensure you added the location ^~ /thumb/ block immediately before the static caching (location ~* \.(jpg...)$) block to prevent static trapping of dynamic thumbnail requests.

docs.title: System Translation (I18n)
docs.text: GeniXCMS uses the industry-standard GNU gettext system for its internationalization (I18n) and localization (L10n). This allows developers and users to translate the entire core interface, including the backend dashboard and system messages, into any language.

docs.title: 📂 Directory Structure
docs.text:
Translation files are stored in the inc/lang/locale/ directory, following the standard gettext hierarchy:

inc/lang/locale/
└── <language_code>/
    └── LC_MESSAGES/
        ├── genixcms.po    (Source translation file)
        ├── genixcms.mo    (Compiled binary file)
        ├── backend.po     (Backend-specific translations)
        └── ...
  • .po (Portable Object): A human-readable text file containing the original strings and their translations.
  • .mo (Machine Object): A compiled binary version of the .po file that the system reads for performance.

docs.title: 🛠️ Translation Workflow
docs.text: To add a new language or modify an existing one:

docs.title: 1. Create the Directory
docs.text:
Create a new folder for your language code (e.g., fr_FR for French) inside inc/lang/locale/. 

mkdir -p inc/lang/locale/fr_FR/LC_MESSAGES/

docs.title: 2. Prepare the .po File
docs.text: Copy an existing genixcms.po file (e.g., from en_US or id_ID) to your new directory.

docs.title: 3. Edit with Poedit
docs.text:
We recommend using Poedit, a popular cross-platform editor for gettext files.

  1. Open your .po file in Poedit.
  2. Translate the strings from the source language to your target language.
  3. Save the file. Poedit will automatically generate the companion .mo binary.

docs.title: 💻 Implementation in Code
docs.text: If you are developing a module or modifying the core, use the following methods to ensure your strings are translatable:

docs.title: 1. In PHP Files
docs.text:
Use the standard gettext function _() or the GeniXCMS wrapper Typo::translate().

// Standard gettext
echo _("Settings Saved Successfully");

// GeniXCMS Wrapper
echo Typo::translate("Add New Post");

docs.title: 2. In Latte Templates
docs.text:
The GeniXCMS template engine is pre-configured with the TranslatorExtension. You can use the underscore tag directly in your .latte files.

<!-- Static string -->
<label>{_ "Username"}</label>

<!-- String with variables -->
<span>{_ "Welcome, %s", $username}</span>

docs.title: ⚙️ Activating a Language
docs.text:
Once your translation files are ready:

  1. Log in to your GeniXCMS Admin Dashboard.
  2. Navigate to Settings > Global Configuration.
  3. Locate the System Language dropdown.
  4. Select your new language and click Save.

The system will now use the .mo files in your specific locale folder to render the interface.

docs.title: 🌍 Multilingual Content vs. System Translation
docs.text:
It is important to distinguish between System Translation and Multilingual Content:

  • System Translation (this guide): Translates the "framework" strings (buttons, labels, menus).
  • Multilingual Content: Handled by the Language class, which allows users to write different versions of the same post/page in multiple languages.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Typo Class — Text processing and translation wrappers.
  • Language Class — Managing multilingual content.
  • BaseControl Class — How the template translator is initialized.

docs.title: File & Image Uploading
docs.text:
Handling file and image uploads in GeniXCMS can be done in two primary ways depending on your use case:

  1. Programmatically via PHP using the native Upload::go() method (ideal for custom forms, profile pictures, or plugin-specific logic).
  2. Visually via the UI using the built-in elFinder media manager (ideal for rich text editors or choosing existing files).

docs.title: 1. Uploading Programmatically (Upload::go())
docs.text: The Upload class provides a streamlined, secure way to handle standard HTML <input type="file"> submissions in your controllers or modules.

docs.title: HTML Form Setup
docs.text:
Always ensure your form has enctype="multipart/form-data".

<form action="" method="post" enctype="multipart/form-data">
    <div class="mb-3">
        <label>Upload Profile Picture</label>
        <input type="file" name="avatar_image" class="form-control" accept="image/png, image/jpeg">
    </div>
    <button type="submit" name="submit_upload" class="btn btn-primary">Upload</button>
</form>

docs.title: Processing the Upload
docs.text:
In your PHP logic, call Upload::go() to process the file. 

if (isset($_POST['submit_upload'])) {
    // Define allowed extensions
    $allowed = ['jpg', 'jpeg', 'png', 'gif', 'webp'];

    // Define the upload path relative to the root (e.g., 'assets/images/avatars/')
    $path = '/assets/images/avatars/';

    // Execute the upload
    // Parameters: input_name, path, allowed_exts, use_unique_name
    $upload = Upload::go('avatar_image', $path, $allowed, true);

    if (isset($upload['error']) && $upload['error'] != '') {
        // Handle Error
        echo "<div class='alert alert-danger'>{$upload['error']}</div>";
    } else {
        // Success
        $filename = $upload['filename'];
        $fullPath = $upload['filepath'];
        $url      = $upload['fileurl'];

        echo "<div class='alert alert-success'>File uploaded to: {$url}</div>";

        // You can now save $url to your database
    }
}

docs.title: Upload::go() Syntax
docs.text:
Upload::go(
    string $input,     // The 'name' attribute of your <input type="file">
    string $path,      // Path to save the file (e.g., '/assets/images/')
    array $allowed,    // Array of allowed extensions (e.g., ['png', 'jpg'])
    bool $uniq = true, // If true, adds a unique hash to prevent overwriting
    int $size = null,  // (Optional) Max file size limit
    int $width = null, // (Optional) Max width for images
    int $height = null // (Optional) Max height for images
);

docs.title: 2. Uploading via the UI (elFinder)
docs.text:
GeniXCMS bundles the elFinder file manager as its core media library. It provides a full GUI for users to browse, upload, and manage files visually.

It is natively integrated into editor instances (like GxEditor and TinyMCE), but you can also spawn it independently.

docs.title: Spawning an elFinder Popup
docs.text:
If you are writing a custom module or theme options panel and want users to "Select an Image" from the media library (or upload a new one), you can trigger the elFinder dialog via AJAX.

1. Create the trigger element and input field: 

<div class="input-group">
    <input type="text" id="my_image_input" name="image_url" class="form-control" placeholder="Select an image...">
    <button type="button" class="btn btn-secondary" onclick="openMediaManager()">
        <i class="bi bi-folder2-open"></i> Browse
    </button>
</div>

2. Trigger elFinder using .dialogelfinder Widget: Note: The url parameter must point to the secure elFinder AJAX endpoint, which requires an active session Token for security.

<script>
function openMediaManager() {
    // We use the native jQuery UI Dialog wrapper for elFinder
    var fm = $("<div/>").dialogelfinder({
        // Generate the secure URL using GeniXCMS Url & Token helpers
        url: "<?= Url::ajax('elfinder/' . Token::create()); ?>",
        lang: "en", 
        width: 840, 
        height: 450,
        destroyOnClose: true,
        // The callback triggered when a user double-clicks an image
        getFileCallback: function (files, fm) {
            // Put the URL into the input field
            $("#my_image_input").val(files.url);

            // Optional: Update an image preview tag
            // $("#post_image_preview").attr("src", files.url).removeClass("d-none");
        },
        commandsOptions: { 
            getfile: { 
                oncomplete: "close", // Close dialog after selection
                folders: false       // Prevent selecting folders
            } 
        }
    }).dialogelfinder("instance");
}
</script>

docs.title: ⚙️ Zero-Backend Upload Processing (How It Works)
docs.text:
One of the biggest advantages of using the elFinder integration is that you do not need to write any backend PHP code to handle the file upload. When you trigger the File Browser using the widget above, the user can visually browse, select, or upload new files directly.

Ketika user melakukan proses upload melalui popup tersebut, proses upload secara terprogram akan di-handle otomatis oleh GeniXCMS di belakang layar:

  1. The client sends an AJAX upload request securely.
  2. The request routes to inc/mod/elfinder/elfinder-ajax.control.php (The elFinder Backend Controller).
  3. The controller validates your active Session Token to block CSRF and unauthorized access.
  4. The backend securely executes the physical file upload directly into the assets/ directory.
  5. The final public URL is returned to the client and injected automatically into your target input field (e.g., #my_image_input) via the getFileCallback.

This means you only need to save the final image_url string to your database, significantly speeding up development time!

docs.title: 3. Background API Uploads (Headless elFinder)
docs.text:
There are scenarios where you want users to click a custom "Add Image" button layout or drop a file into an editor without ever seeing the file manager dialog popup.

You can accomplish this programmatically by sending an AJAX request directly to the elFinder Controller (using its API) with your file payload.

docs.title: Custom HTML Trigger
docs.text:
<button type="button" onclick="document.getElementById('hidden_file_input').click()" class="aspect-video rounded-2xl border-2 border-dashed border-slate-200 flex flex-col items-center justify-center text-slate-400 hover:border-primary hover:text-primary hover:bg-primary/5 transition-all outline-none">
    <span class="material-symbols-outlined text-[32px]">add_a_photo</span>
    <span class="text-[10px] font-black uppercase tracking-widest mt-2">Add Image</span>
</button>
<input type="file" id="hidden_file_input" class="d-none" onchange="uploadDirectAsset(this.files[0])">

docs.title: The AJAX Upload Logic
docs.text:
The function first makes an init request to retrieve the current working directory (cwd.hash), then posts the file content payload.

<script>
function uploadDirectAsset(file) {
    if (!file) return;

    // Secure URL mapping to the elFinder endpoint
    var elfinderUrl = "<?= Url::ajax('elfinder/' . Token::create()); ?>";
    var sep = elfinderUrl.indexOf('?') === -1 ? '?' : '&';

    // Step 1: Initialize the API to get the target directory hash
    $.ajax({ url: elfinderUrl + sep + 'cmd=open&init=1&target=', type: 'GET', dataType: 'json' })
     .done(function(initData) {
         if (!initData || !initData.cwd) {
             console.error('Failed to init elfinder API');
             return;
         }

         // Extract the hash ID of the root directory
         var targetHash = initData.cwd.hash;

         // Step 2: Build the multipart payload
         var fd = new FormData();
         fd.append('cmd', 'upload');
         fd.append('target', targetHash);
         fd.append('upload[]', file);

         // Step 3: Execute the background upload
         $.ajax({
             url: elfinderUrl, 
             data: fd, 
             cache: false, 
             contentType: false, 
             processData: false, 
             type: 'POST',
             success: function(data) {
                 var parsed = typeof data === 'string' ? JSON.parse(data) : data;

                 // If successful, 'added' array contains the new file metadata
                 if (parsed.added && parsed.added.length > 0) {
                     var fileUrl = parsed.added[0].url;
                     console.log("Upload Success: ", fileUrl);

                     // Example: Insert the image into the DOM
                     // $("#preview_image").attr('src', fileUrl);
                 } else if (parsed.error) {
                     console.error("Upload Error: ", parsed.error);
                 }
             },
             error: function(jqXHR, textStatus, errorThrown) {
                 console.error(textStatus + " " + errorThrown);
             }
         });
     });
}
</script>

docs.title: Extending the Admin Navigation Menu
docs.text: GeniXCMS v2.0.0 introduces a programmable admin menu system via the AdminMenu class. This allows modules and themes to add, remove, or reorder navigation items in the admin panel sidebar and top navbar without modifying any core file.

docs.title: 🏗️ Architecture Overview
docs.text:
The admin navigation is driven by a central registry. When the system boots, it:

  1. Loads all active Modules (new Mod())
  2. Loads the active Theme (new Theme())
  3. Fires the init hook
  4. Initializes AdminMenu (new AdminMenu()) — which registers core items and processes any items modules have already queued

This means you can safely call AdminMenu::add() from anywhere in your module or theme's function.php or index.php.

docs.title: 🔌 Registration in a Module
docs.text: The recommended location to register your admin menu items is your module's function.php file (loaded automatically on every page load when the module is active).

docs.title: Minimal Example
docs.text:
// inc/mod/my_shop/function.php

AdminMenu::add([
    'id'       => 'my_shop',
    'label'    => _('My Shop'),
    'icon'     => 'bi bi-cart3',
    'url'      => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=my_shop',
    'access'   => 3,
    'position' => 'external',
    'order'    => 10,
]);

docs.title: Full Example with Sub-Menu
docs.text:
// inc/mod/inventory/function.php

AdminMenu::add([
    'id'       => 'inventory',
    'label'    => _('Inventory'),
    'icon'     => 'bi bi-box-seam-fill',
    'url'      => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=inventory',
    'access'   => 2,           // Editor and above
    'position' => 'external',
    'order'    => 20,
    'children' => [
        [
            'label'  => _('Products'),
            'icon'   => 'bi bi-boxes',
            'url'    => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=inventory&tab=products',
            'access' => 2,
        ],
        [
            'label'  => _('Orders'),
            'icon'   => 'bi bi-receipt',
            'url'    => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=inventory&tab=orders',
            'access' => 2,
        ],
        [
            'label'  => _('Stock Report'),
            'icon'   => 'bi bi-bar-chart-line',
            'url'    => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=inventory&tab=report',
            'access' => 1,    // Supervisor only for reports
        ],
    ],
]);

docs.title: 🎨 Registration in a Theme
docs.text:
If your theme provides its own admin utilities (e.g., a Theme Options shortcut), register the item in your theme's function.php.

// inc/themes/my_theme/function.php

AdminMenu::add([
    'id'       => 'my_theme_options',
    'label'    => _('Theme Options'),
    'icon'     => 'bi bi-brush-fill',
    'url'      => 'index.php?page=themes&act=options',
    'access'   => 0,          // Administrator only
    'position' => 'external',
    'order'    => 5,
]);

docs.title: 📐 Menu Positions
docs.text:
Choose the right position to place your item in the correct region:

Position Sidebar Section Top Navbar Behavior main Under "Main Navigation" Shown as top-level nav links management Under "Management" Shown as top-level or dropdown settings Under Settings group Shown inside "Settings" dropdown external Under "External" Shown inside "External" dropdown

[!TIP] Best Practice: Use external for module-specific items. Reserve main and management for items that have broad, primary importance across the whole site.

docs.title: 🌲 Adding Sub-Menus to Existing Items
docs.text: If you want to inject a sub-menu into an existing parent item (e.g., adding a "Draft Store" link under the "Posts" menu or a "Custom Settings" under "Settings"), use AdminMenu::addChild().

docs.title: Adding a Single Sub-Menu
docs.text:
// Add a custom link under the core "Posts" menu
AdminMenu::addChild('posts', [
    'label'  => _('My Post Type'),
    'url'    => 'index.php?page=posts&type=custom',
    'icon'   => 'bi bi-star',
    'access' => 4,   // Author level and above
]);

docs.title: Adding Multiple Sub-Menus at Once
docs.text:
Use AdminMenu::addChildren() to inject a batch of items into a parent.

AdminMenu::addChildren('settings', [
    [
        'label' => _('Store Settings'),
        'url'   => 'index.php?page=settings&tab=store',
    ],
    [
        'label' => _('API Configuration'),
        'url'   => 'index.php?page=settings&tab=api',
    ]
]);

[!TIP] Parent IDs: Common core parent IDs include posts, pages, users, settings, themes, modules, permissions, comments, and media.

docs.title: 🔄 Removing Core Menu Items
docs.text:
You can remove any core menu item using its id. This is useful for building specialized or restricted admin panels.

// Remove core items your custom installation does not need
AdminMenu::remove('comments');    // Remove Comments from sidebar
AdminMenu::remove('permissions'); // Remove ACL Manager link

[!CAUTION] Removing core items may confuse users who expect standard navigation. Only do this if your module or client installation explicitly requires a simplified interface.

docs.title: 🔢 Controlling Order
docs.text:
All core items use order values that are multiples of 10. Use values between them to inject items precisely:

order: 10  → Posts
order: 15  → 📌 My Custom Item  ← injected between Posts and Pages
order: 20  → Pages
order: 30  → Comments

AdminMenu::add([
    'id'    => 'newsletter',
    'label' => _('Newsletter'),
    'icon'  => 'bi bi-envelope-paper',
    'url'   => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=newsletter',
    'position' => 'main',
    'order' => 15,   // Appears between Posts (10) and Pages (20)
]);

docs.title: 🧩 Using Access Control
docs.text:
The access key controls which user levels can see the menu item. It uses the same numeric role system as User::access():

Value Role 0 Administrator 1 Supervisor 2 Editor 3 Author 4 Contributor 5 VIP Member 6 General Member 
// Only administrators see this item
AdminMenu::add([
    ...
    'access' => 0,
]);

// Authors and above
AdminMenu::add([
    ...
    'access' => 3,
]);

Child items can also have their own access level, which overrides the parent:

'children' => [
    ['label' => 'Public Stats',  'url' => '...', 'access' => 4], // Authors+
    ['label' => 'Private Stats', 'url' => '...', 'access' => 0], // Admin only
],

docs.title: 🔌 Using the init Hook (Advanced)
docs.text:
For items that depend on runtime conditions (e.g., feature flags or options), you can defer registration to the init hook:

// inc/mod/my_module/function.php

Hooks::attach('init', function() {
    if (Options::v('my_module_premium') === 'on') {
        AdminMenu::add([
            'id'       => 'my_module_premium',
            'label'    => _('Premium Features'),
            'icon'     => 'bi bi-star-fill',
            'url'      => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=my_module&tab=premium',
            'position' => 'external',
            'order'    => 30,
        ]);
    }
});

[!IMPORTANT] The init hook fires before new AdminMenu(), so items registered via init are available when the core menu is being built.

docs.title: 📋 Complete Reference
docs.text: Method Description AdminMenu::add(array $item) Register a new menu item. AdminMenu::remove(string $id) Unregister a menu item by ID. AdminMenu::getItems(?string $pos) Get sorted array of items, optionally filtered by position. AdminMenu::renderSidebar(?string $pos) Output sidebar <li> HTML for given position. AdminMenu::renderTopNav(?string $pos) Output top navbar <li> HTML for given position.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• AdminMenu Class Reference — Full API documentation.
  • Create a Module — How to build a complete GeniXCMS module.
  • Create a Theme — How to build a custom admin-aware theme.
  • Using Hooks — Deferred registration using the init hook.

docs.title: How to Use the Internal Ajax API
docs.text:
GeniXCMS provides an internal JSON-based API accessible at /ajax/api/. This is primarily intended for internal frontend interactions (like search suggestions or theme widgets) and requires a valid CSRF session token.

[!IMPORTANT] For external applications, mobile apps, or headless CMS integrations, please use the RESTful API (v1) which uses secure API Keys and standard REST terminology.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text:
{site_url}/ajax/api/{TOKEN}?action={action}

For non-Smart URL mode: 

{site_url}/index.php?ajax=api&token={TOKEN}&action={action}

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: The API uses CSRF tokens for protected endpoints. Public endpoints do not require a token.

docs.title: Getting a Token
docs.text:
The current session token is available as the TOKEN constant in templates:

{* In a Latte template *}
<script>
    const API_TOKEN = '{TOKEN}';
</script>

In PHP: 

$token = TOKEN; // Available globally after session is initialized

docs.title: recent_posts (Public)
docs.text:
Returns the most recent posts with metadata and thumbnail URLs.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=recent_posts&num=5&type=post

Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description num int 5 Number of posts to return type string 'post' Content type: 'post' or 'page' cat int (none) Filter by category ID

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        {
            "id": 42,
            "title": "My Post Title",
            "url": "https://example.com/my-post.html",
            "date": "26 March 2026",
            "author": "admin",
            "author_url": "https://example.com/author/admin/",
            "category": "Technology",
            "category_url": "https://example.com/category/1/technology/",
            "image": "https://example.com/thumb/type/square/size/300/...",
            "excerpt": "A short preview of the post content..."
        }
    ]
}

docs.title: categories (Public)
docs.text:
Returns all categories of a given type.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=categories&type=post

Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description type string 'post' Taxonomy type: 'post' or 'page'

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        { "id": 1, "name": "Technology", "slug": "technology" },
        { "id": 2, "name": "Travel", "slug": "travel" }
    ]
}

docs.title: authors (Public)
docs.text:
Returns a unique list of usernames who have published content.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=authors

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        { "author": "admin" },
        { "author": "p_wijayanto" }
    ]
}

docs.title: tags (Token Required)
docs.text:
Returns tag suggestions matching a search term. Used for autocomplete.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=tags&term=php

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description term string Prefix to search for matching tag names

docs.title: JavaScript Usage Example
docs.text:
const SITE_URL = 'https://example.com';
const TOKEN = '...'; // from your Latte template

// Fetch recent posts
fetch(`${SITE_URL}/ajax/api/${TOKEN}?action=recent_posts&num=6`)
    .then(res => res.json())
    .then(({ status, data }) => {
        if (status !== 'success') return;
        data.forEach(post => {
            console.log(post.title, post.url);
        });
    });

docs.title: Error Responses
docs.text: Scenario Response Invalid or missing token (protected action) {"status":"error","message":"Token not exist or invalid"} Unknown action {"status":"error","message":"Unknown action"}

docs.title: Adding Custom API Actions
docs.text:
You can extend the API from a module by listening to the api-ajax controller. Create a custom Ajax controller at:

inc/mod/my-module/ajax/myaction-ajax.control.php

Or attach to an existing hook if available:

// In your module class constructor:
Hooks::attach('api_action_myaction', ['MyModule', 'handleApiAction']);

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Control Layer Reference — How AJAX routes are dispatched.
  • Token Class — How CSRF tokens are generated and validated.
  • Http Class — For making outbound HTTP requests from the server side.

docs.title: Using Virtual Cron
docs.text:
GeniXCMS v2.0.0 introduces the Cron class, allowing modules and themes to schedule recurring or one-time tasks without needing a system-level crontab.

GeniXCMS's cron is "virtual"—it checks for due tasks on every visitor's request.

docs.title: 🚀 Basic Usage
docs.text:
To use the Cron system, you typically:

  1. Attach a function to a custom hook.
  2. Schedule that hook using the Cron class.

docs.title: 🐘 1. Attach a Hook
docs.text:
Your module or theme should define what happens when the cron runs:

// in inc/mod/my_module/function.php
Hooks::attach('my_daily_sync', function($args) {
    // Perform sync logic here...
    // $args will be an array of arguments passed during scheduling
});

docs.title: 🗓️ 2. Schedule the Task
docs.text:
It is best to schedule the task once when the system starts up or during module activation:

// Schedule a daily task
if (!Cron::getNext('my_daily_sync')) { // Check if already scheduled
    Cron::schedule(time(), 'daily', 'my_daily_sync', ['force' => true]);
}

docs.title: 🔍 Checking Scheduled Tasks
docs.text:
You can check when the next run for a specific hook is scheduled:

$next_run = Cron::getNext('my_daily_sync');
if ($next_run) {
    echo "Next run: " . date('Y-m-d H:i:s', $next_run);
}

docs.title: 🗑️ Clearing Schedules
docs.text:
If you no longer need a scheduled task (e.g., when a module is deactivated), you should clear it:

Cron::clear('my_daily_sync');

docs.title: 🔧 One-Time Events
docs.text:
To run a task exactly once in the future:

// Run 30 minutes from now
Cron::scheduleOnce(time() + 1800, 'send_follow_up_email', ['user_id' => 45]);

docs.title: ➕ Adding Custom Intervals
docs.text:
By default, hourly, twicedaily, daily, and weekly are available. You can add more using the cron_schedules filter:

Hooks::attach('cron_schedules', function($schedules) {
    $schedules['every_five_mins'] = [
        'interval' => 300,
        'display'  => _('Every 5 Minutes')
    ];
    return $schedules;
});

docs.title: ⚙️ Triggering via Real System Cron (Better Performance)
docs.text:
While virtual cron works on every request, it can cause slight delays for visitors or might not run at all if there is no traffic.

For high-traffic or mission-critical sites, you can trigger the cron via a real crontab on your server:

# Add to your system crontab (e.g. crontab -e)
* * * * * curl https://your-site.com/index.php > /dev/null 2>&1

Or you can create a dedicated PHP script that only runs Cron::run().

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Cron Class Reference — Full API documentation.
  • Using Hooks — How hooks work in GeniXCMS.
  • Create a Module — Best practices for module initialization.

docs.title: Guidelines for Using the Editor Class
docs.text: The Editor class in GeniXCMS is a centralized registry for WYSIWYG editor engines. It enables modularity by allowing developers to register custom text editors through module initialization. This system decouples the specific editor implementation from the theme templates, ensuring cleaner code and improved resource management.

docs.title: 1. Registering an Editor
docs.text: To register a new editor engine, use the Editor::register() method. This informs the system about the editor’s availability and provides a callback for asset injection.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: Editor::register(string $id, string $name, callable $callback);

docs.title: Example: Registering from a Module
docs.text:
public static function init()
{
    // Register the editor with its setup callback
    Editor::register('my_pro_editor', 'Professional Suite Editor', [self::class, 'setupEditor']);
}

/**
 * The callback is only executed if 'my_pro_editor' is selected in System Settings.
 */
public static function setupEditor()
{
    // 1. Register assets with dependencies and context
    Asset::register('my-editor-css', 'css', Site::$url . '/inc/mod/my-mod/css/editor.css');
    Asset::register('my-editor-js', 'js', Site::$url . '/inc/mod/my-mod/js/editor.js', 'footer', ['jquery']);

    // 2. Enqueue the assets
    Asset::enqueue(['my-editor-css', 'my-editor-js']);

    // 3. Inject initialization scripts as a raw asset
    Asset::load('my-editor-init', 'raw', '<script>/* activation logic */</script>');
}

docs.title: 2. Managing Editor Assets
docs.text: The setup callback should exclusively use the Asset Class for registration and enqueuing. Modern GeniXCMS 2.0.0 engines utilize this to prevent direct echo operations, which can disrupt the HTML rendering sequence.

docs.title: Key Considerations:
docs.text:
• Dependency Chains: Ensure your editor JS depends on core frameworks like jquery or bootstrap.
  • Positioning: Load lightweight CSS in the header and heavy initialization scripts in the footer.
  • Deduplication: The Asset class will automatically prevent the same script from being rendered twice if it's used by multiple components.

docs.title: 3. Using the Editor in Themes
docs.text: Themes trigger the initialization of the active editor engine by calling Theme::editor(). This method prepares the environment and tells the system that the current view contains textareas with the .editor class.

docs.title: Theme Template (Latte)
docs.text:
{Theme::editor('full', '400')|noescape}

<div class="mb-3">
    <label>Content</label>
    <textarea name="content" class="form-control editor">
        {$post->content|noescape}
    </textarea>
</div>

When Theme::editor() is executed, the GeniXCMS core will automatically look up the active editor engine and fire its registration callback, ensuring that only the relevant assets for your selected editor (e.g., GxEditor, Summernote) are loaded.

docs.title: 4. Supporting Legacy Hooks
docs.text:
For backward compatibility, the Editor system still supports the editor_type_options filter. If used, the system will attempt to detect the active engine based on the filtered options. However, it's highly recommended to use the Editor::register() method for new modules to take full advantage of the automated callback execution.

Hooks::filter('editor_type_options', function($options) {
    if (!isset($options['my_legacy_editor'])) {
        $options['my_legacy_editor'] = 'Legacy Support Editor';
    }
    return $options;
});

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Asset Management Class API
  • Editor Class API
  • GxEditor Block Editor Guide
  • How to Use Hooks

docs.title: GxEditor: The official GeniXCMS Block Editor
docs.text: GxEditor is a modern, modular block-based editor bundled with GeniXCMS. It is inspired by Gutenberg and EditorJS, providing a seamless authoring experience while maintaining clean, structured HTML output.

docs.title: 1. Enabling GxEditor
docs.text: To use GxEditor on a page or post, ensure it is selected as the active editor in Settings > System > Content Settings.

docs.title: Theme Integration
docs.text:
In your theme, call the editor setup before rendering your textarea:

{Theme::editor('full', '400')|noescape}

<textarea name="content" class="editor">
    {$post->content|noescape}
</textarea>

docs.title: 2. Standard Blocks
docs.text:
GxEditor comes with a variety of built-in blocks:

  • Text: Paragraphs and headers (H1-H4).
  • Media: Images (integrated with elFinder) and Icons.
  • Layout: 2-Column Grids, 2x2 Grids, and Cards.
  • Dynamic: Recent Posts, Random Posts, and Table of Contents (TOC).
  • Structure: Tables, Lists (UL/OL), and Icon-marker Lists.
  • Section: Price Comparison Table with monthly/yearly switch.
  • Advanced: Math Equations (KaTeX integration).

docs.title: Math Equations
docs.text:
GxEditor features a built-in Math Editor powered by KaTeX.

  • Toolbar Icon: Look for the Sigma-Root symbol (Σ√).
  • Interaction: Opens a modal where you can type LaTeX code with a live preview.
  • Editing: Double-clicking an existing math equation in the editor will re-open the editor modal for quick adjustments.

docs.title: Professional Table Wizard
docs.text:
Legacy prompt-based table insertion has been replaced with a proper modal wizard.

  • Configuration: Set rows, columns, and header preference.
  • Themes: Toggle Bootstrap table-striped and table-bordered styles directly from the insertion modal.
  • Contextual Actions: Right-click (or use the toolbar dropdown in Classic Mode) to add/remove rows and columns dynamically.

docs.title: 3. Creating Custom Blocks
docs.text: One of GxEditor's strongest features is its extensibility. You can register custom blocks from any module.

docs.title: Step 1: Register the Block (PHP)
docs.text:
Use the GxEditor::registerBlock() method in your module's init() or constructor.

GxEditor::registerBlock('my_custom_block', [
    'label' => 'My Feature',
    'icon'  => 'bi bi-lightning',
    'desc'  => 'Add a lightning-fast feature block'
]);

docs.title: Step 2: Implement Rendering (JavaScript)
docs.text:
The actual rendering and serialization of blocks are handled by JavaScript in the browser. You need to hook into the GxEditor Javascript object.

Create a file my-block-editor.js and enqueue it.

// Register the block in the JS layer
GxEditor.registerBlock('my_custom_block', {
    placeholder: 'Enter your lightning text...',
    // (Optional) Custom render logic
    render: function(state, block, content, wrapper) {
        content.classList.add('my-feature-rendered');
        content.contentEditable = 'true';
        content.innerHTML = block.content || 'Default text';
    }
});

docs.title: Step 3: Handle Serialization (Parser)
docs.text: If your block needs specialized shortcode or HTML output, you must ensure the parser knows how to handle it. By default, GxEditor treats custom blocks as simple text containers if no special logic is provided.

docs.title: 4. GxEditor Architecture
docs.text:
GxEditor is built as a set of modular JS components:

  • gxeditor.js: Core state management and block registration.
  • gxeditor-renderer.js: Converts block JSON into editable DOM elements.
  • gxeditor-parser.js: Converts DOM/JSON into Final HTML/Shortcodes and vice versa.
  • gxeditor-ui.js: Toolbars, pickers, and context menus.

docs.title: The State Object
docs.text:
Each editor instance has a state object containing:

  • blocks: An array of block objects {id, type, content, ...}.
  • shell: The main DOM container.
  • textarea: The source textarea being synced.

docs.title: 5. Advanced: Nested Editors
docs.text:
GxEditor supports nesting editors within blocks (like in the Grid or Card blocks). This is achieved by creating a new GxEditor instance for a specific child element.

// Example: Creating a nested editor inside a block render function
var subTa = document.createElement('textarea');
subTa.className = 'editor gxb-nested-editor';
content.appendChild(subTa);

// Initialize after a tick
setTimeout(function() {
    window.initShell(subTa);
}, 0);

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Asset Management
  • Create a Module
  • Editor Class

docs.title: How to Use Hooks
docs.text:
Hooks are the primary extension mechanism in GeniXCMS. They allow themes and modules to add functionality or modify output without editing core files.

There are two types of hooks:

  • Actions — Execute a function at a specific point (no return value expected).
  • Filters — Modify a value and return the modified result.

docs.title: Attaching an Action
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::attach(string $hook_name, callable $callback, int $priority = 10);

// Attach a function to the 'footer_load_lib' hook
Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyTheme', 'loadAssets']);

// Or with a closure
Hooks::attach('post_submit_add_action', function($data) {
    // Send a notification email when a post is added
    Mail::send([
        'to'      => 'admin@example.com',
        'subject' => 'New Post Published',
        'message' => 'A new post was just published.',
    ]);
});

docs.title: Running an Action
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::run(string $hook_name, mixed $data = '');

// In a theme template (Latte)
{Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape}

// In PHP code
Hooks::run('post_submit_add_action', $_POST);

docs.title: Attaching a Filter
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::filter(string $hook_name, mixed $value);

The attached function must accept the value and return a (modified) value.

// Attach a filter to modify post titles
Hooks::attach('post_title_filter', function($title) {
    return strtoupper($title); // uppercase all titles
});

docs.title: Applying a Filter
docs.text:
// In PHP
$title = Hooks::filter('post_title_filter', $post->title);

// In a Latte template
{Hooks::filter('post_title_filter', $post->title)|noescape}

docs.title: Action Hooks
docs.text: Hook Trigger Point $data Type footer_load_lib Just before </body> in every page (none) header_load_meta Inside <head> of every page (none) init Standard system startup finished (none) post_submit_add_action After a new post is saved $_POST array post_sqldel_action After a post is deleted from database int $id admin_sidebar_start Before the first admin sidebar menu (none) admin_sidebar_end After the last admin sidebar menu (none) theme_activated_action After a theme is successfully activated string $theme_name admin_page_dashboard_action On the admin dashboard $data array admin_footer_action At the bottom of every admin page (none) user_login_action After a successful user login $data array user_register_action After a new user registers $data array post_param_form_bottom At the bottom of the main post form area $data array post_param_form_sidebar At the bottom of the post form sidebar $data array page_param_form_bottom At the bottom of the main page form area $data array page_param_form_sidebar At the bottom of the page form sidebar $data array

docs.title: Filter Hooks
docs.text: Hook What It Filters post_title_filter Post title string before display post_pre_insert_filter Modify entire post data before inserting to DB post_pre_update_filter Modify entire post data before updating in DB user_pre_insert_filter Modify entire user data before creating user user_pre_update_filter Modify entire user data before updating post_submit_title_filter Title string during save (sanitize) post_submit_content_filter Content string during save (sanitize) post_content_filter Post body HTML before render system_security_headers_args CSP rules array (directives and sources) search_type_filter Array of post types to include in Search queries breadcrumbs_filter Array of breadcrumb items (label and URL) post_url Array containing the generated URL and post ID widget_locations Associative array of available widget zones/locations editor_type_options Associative array of available editor engines

docs.title: Priority
docs.text:
Attach multiple callbacks to the same hook. Lower priority numbers run first.

Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['Analytics', 'load'], 5);  // runs first
Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['Ads', 'load'], 20);        // runs later

docs.title: Registering Hooks in a Theme
docs.text:
For a modern theme implementation, put all hook registrations inside a dedicated Theme Class within your function.php. This approach keeps your code organized and allows for clean access to theme utilities like the Asset manager.

<?php
class MyModernTheme
{
    public function __construct()
    {
        // 1. Efficiently load assets (Alternative to footer_load_lib echo)
        $this->setupAssets();

        // 2. Attach filters (Title & Content)
        Hooks::attach('post_title_filter',   [self::class, 'injectTitleIcon'], 10);
        Hooks::attach('post_content_filter', [self::class, 'formatContent'], 20);

        // 3. Register Widget zones specifically for this theme
        Widget::addLocation('sidebar_landing', 'Landing Page Sidebar');
    }

    private function setupAssets()
    {
        // Load default Bootstrap from CDN
        Asset::enqueue(['bootstrap-css', 'bootstrap-js', 'bootstrap-icons']);

        // Register theme-specific JS/CSS
        Asset::register('theme-css', 'css', Url::theme() . '/css/style.css', 'header');
        Asset::register('theme-js',  'js',  Url::theme() . '/js/app.js',     'footer', ['jquery']);

        Asset::enqueue(['theme-css', 'theme-js']);
    }

    public static function injectTitleIcon($title)
    {
        // Add a book icon to every post title
        return '📖 ' . $title;
    }

    public static function formatContent($content)
    {
        // Automatically add a "Related Posts" section after content
        $related = '<hr><p class="fw-bold">Don\'t forget to share this post!</p>';
        return $content . $related;
    }
}
new MyModernTheme();

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class API — Full method reference.
  • Create a Theme — Where to register hooks in a theme.
  • Create a Module — Using hooks in modules.

docs.title: Creating and Using Shortcodes in GeniXCMS
docs.text:
Shortcodes are a powerful feature in GeniXCMS version 2.0.0 that allow you to create dynamic, parameterized content within posts and pages. Unlike plain HTML, shortcodes are processed on the server at render time, making them ideal for injecting complex UI components like Table of Contents, Product Sliders, or Recent Posts.

This guide explains how to programmatically create and use shortcodes in your Modules and Themes.

docs.title: 🏗️ Basic Shortcode Architecture
docs.text:
A shortcode is a tag wrapped in square brackets, such as [my_tag key="value"]. To implement a shortcode in GeniXCMS, you need two things:

  1. A Callback Function: Logic that generates the HTML for the shortcode.
  2. A Hook Connection: Attaching your logic to the post_content_filter filter.

docs.title: 🛠️ Step 1: Create a Callback Function
docs.text:
In your module's index.php or theme's functions.php:

function my_module_render_shortcode($content) {
    // We use the Shortcode::parse library class
